crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature detects the presence of a number of Windows API functionality often seen within embedded executables. When this signature alerts on an executable, it is not an indication of malicious behavior. However, if seen firing in other file types, deeper investigation may be warranted.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature fires on the presence of Base64 encoded URI prefixes (http:// and https://) across any file. The simple presence of such strings is not inherently an indicator of malicious content, but is worth further investigation.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

android.permission.VIBRATE.full_description: Allows the app to control the vibrator.

android.permission.RECEIVE_BOOT_COMPLETE.full_description:
Allows the app to
        have itself started as soon as the system has finished booting.
        This can make it take longer to start the phone and allow the
        app to slow down the overall phone by always running.

android.permission.ACCESS_NETWORK_STATE.full_description:
Allows the app to view
      information about network connections such as which networks exist and are
      connected.

android.permission.WAKE_LOCK.full_description: Allows the app to prevent the phone from going to sleep.

android.permission.INTERNET.full_description:
Allows the app to create
     network sockets and use custom network protocols. The browser and other
     applications provide means to send data to the internet, so this
     permission is not required to send data to the internet.

android.permission.FOREGROUND_SERVICE.full_description: Allows the app to make use of foreground services.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature detects a PDF file that contains JavaScript. JavaScript can be used to customize PDFs by implementing objects, methods, and properties. While not inherently malicious, embedding JavaScript inside of a PDF is often used for malicious purposes such as malware delivery or exploitation.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

sigma_analysis_results.rule_description:
Detects an svchost process spawning an instance of an office application. This happens when the initial word application creates an instance of one of the Office COM objects such as 'Word.Application', 'Excel.Application', etc.
This can be used by malicious actors to create malicious Office documents with macros on the fly. (See vba2clr project in the references)
sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Suspicious New Instance Of An Office COM Object

trid.file_type: Word Microsoft Office Open XML Format document

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Office Application Initiated Network Connection To Non-Local IP
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description:
Detects an office application (Word, Excel, PowerPoint)  that initiate a network connection to a non-private IP addresses.
This rule aims to detect traffic similar to one seen exploited in CVE-2021-42292.
This rule will require an initial baseline and tuning that is specific to your organization.
sigma_analysis_results.rule_author: Christopher Peacock '@securepeacock', SCYTHE '@scythe_io', Florian Roth (Nextron Systems), Tim Shelton

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
The document is a legitimate research report from the cybersecurity organization Citizen Lab, titled "Tainted Leaks Disinformation and Phishing With a Russian Nexus." The visual content is professional, academic, and lacks any red flags associated with malicious documents, such as urgency cues or impersonation. Its purpose is to analyze a state-sponsored phishing campaign, not to conduct one.

The internal technical analysis corroborates this assessment. Although it identified a large number of URIs (140) and numerous stream decoding errors, it found no evidence of a viable execution chain. Key malicious components, such as automatic action triggers (`/OpenAction`) or embedded JavaScript, are absent. The presence of many URLs is consistent with the document's nature as a research paper citing evidence.

In conclusion, the file is a static, informational document. The technical anomalies are not linked to any malicious functionality, and the visual context confirms the document's benign nature as a public security report.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
The document presents a conflicting profile. Visually, it appears to be a legitimate academic paper with a credible author, authentic affiliations, and appropriate citations. The text, URLs, and overall presentation do not contain any red flags typically associated with malicious content.

However, the internal technical analysis reveals a structure that is highly indicative of a malicious file. The PDF is configured with an `/OpenAction` trigger, designed to execute code automatically when the document is opened. This trigger is paired with 14 embedded JavaScript objects. This combination creates a potential execution chain, a common technique for deploying exploits or malware through PDF files.

While the benign visual layer could be a social engineering tactic to establish trust, the suspicious underlying architecture cannot be ignored. The presence of an automated execution mechanism, even without the specific code content for analysis, is a significant risk. Therefore, the file is judged to be suspicious.

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
The technical analysis identified significant structural anomalies, including a very high object count and 445 streams that could not be decoded. These issues suggest the file may be corrupted or was created using a non-standard process. However, the analysis crucially found no automatic execution triggers, such as /OpenAction or document-level JavaScript. In the absence of an execution vector, any potential code within the undecodable streams is rendered inert, as it has no mechanism to run.

This assessment is supported by the visual layer analysis, which found the document to be a legitimate ebook from the publisher Farrar, Straus and Giroux (Macmillan). All visible branding, text, and URLs appear authentic and consistent with the publisher. The document contains no social engineering tactics, urgency cues, or other red flags typically associated with malicious content.

Considering both reports, the file is determined to be benign. The lack of a technical execution chain outweighs the structural anomalies, and the legitimate appearance of the content confirms the file's non-malicious nature. The internal errors are most likely attributable to file corruption or generation issues rather than malicious design.

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Suspicious New Instance Of An Office COM Object
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description:
Detects an svchost process spawning an instance of an office application. This happens when the initial word application creates an instance of one of the Office COM objects such as 'Word.Application', 'Excel.Application', etc.
This can be used by malicious actors to create malicious Office documents with macros on the fly. (See vba2clr project in the references)
Detects the creation of a new office macro files on the systems

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
The macro starts by defining a function called `TCNGUziO`. This function takes a hexadecimal string as input and converts it into a byte array using `Microsoft.XMLDOM` object.

Another function named `MacroMeter` is then defined. It declares a variable `rSaXqUGDeX` and assigns a long, concatenated hexadecimal string to it. This string is then processed by the `TCNGUziO` function to generate a byte array. The macro then creates an `ADODB.Stream` object, writes the byte array into it, and saves it to a file named "pfotolauncf.exe" in the "C:\ProgramData\" directory. After a delay of 3 seconds, the macro uses `CreateObject(\"Excel.Application\").Wait` to execute the newly written file.

The macro also contains a subroutine named `autoclose`.  This subroutine uses a byte array to construct the string  "Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run". It then employs `StdRegProv` to create a string value named  "PersfromOffice" within this registry key. The value data is set to "C:\ProgramData\pfotolauncf.exe".

Additionally, the macro defines two other functions: `DZMPILqE13Y` and `CqeKEQmEZ`. `DZMPILqE13Y` sets the `ShowHiddenText` property of the active document view to `True`, revealing any hidden text in the document. `CqeKEQmEZ` checks for the presence of inline shapes in the document. If an inline shape of type `wdInlineShapePicture` is found, and its alternative text contains "jpg", the shape is deleted. The document is then saved.

Finally, the `AutoOpen` subroutine is called when the document is opened. It executes `MacroMeter`, `CqeKEQmEZ`, and `DZMPILqE13Y` sequentially.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs
